figures.gloss: The perfected disciple of the Theravada path. A mortal who has permanently extinguished the 'three poisons' of greed, hatred, and delusion, shattering the chains of dependent origination to attain Nibbana and end the cycle of rebirth.
figures.facet: It represents the zenith of individual renunciation. Liberation is won strictly by self-effort and forensic dismantling of personal identity, rejecting any reliance on cosmic saviors or divine grace.
figures.provenance: Pali Canon (Tipitaka), particularly the Dhammapada and Sutta Pitaka.

figures.gloss: The supreme paradigm of the Mahayana vehicle. An awakening being who deliberately forestalls their own final dissolution into Nirvana in order to remain in Samsara until every single sentient being is liberated.
figures.facet: It radically redefines enlightenment from a personal exit-strategy to infinite, active compassion. The Bodhisattva does not escape the burning house of the world, but returns to it willingly.

figures.gloss: The ultimate metaphysical reality in Mahayana thought. It is the profound realization that all phenomena are radically devoid of inherent, independent essence, existing only as a fleeting web of mutual interdependence.
figures.facet: It is not a nihilistic void, but the very condition of possibility. Because things lack rigid, permanent essence, transformation and liberation are possible. It weaponizes non-existence to destroy clinging.

figures.gloss: The luminous, innate potential for Buddhahood residing at the core of every sentient being, completely untouched by the temporary defilements of karma and ignorance.
figures.facet: It borders on a positive ontology within a tradition famous for negation. It suggests enlightenment is not built from scratch, but simply uncovered—a controversial shift from early Buddhist deconstruction.

figures.gloss: The personal meditational deity in Vajrayana Tantra. It is not an external creator-god, but a pure, holographic expression of the practitioner's own latent enlightened mind, used as a fierce anchor for cognitive transformation.
figures.facet: It bypasses gradual asceticism through 'Deity Yoga.' By fiercely visualizing oneself already possessing the body, speech, and mind of the Buddha, the practitioner short-circuits the eons required for conventional awakening.
figures.provenance: Tibetan Buddhist Tantras (e.g., Guhyasamaja, Hevajra Tantra).

figures.gloss: The indispensable human preceptor of the Vajrayana path. The Lama is viewed not merely as a scholarly guide, but as the literal, living embodiment of the Dharmakaya and all Buddhas across time.
figures.facet: Devotion to the Guru effectively supersedes devotion to the historical Buddha. Without the direct, esoteric transmission of 'lung' (authorization) from a living Lama, Tantric texts are considered inert and highly dangerous.
figures.provenance: Tibetan Lamrim texts, Asvaghosa's 'Fifty Verses of Guru Devotion'.

figures.gloss: The spontaneous, unshakeable wish to attain complete enlightenment specifically for the benefit of all beings. It manifests in two forms: relative (the altruistic intention) and absolute (direct insight into emptiness).
figures.facet: It is the sole alchemical ingredient that separates a generic good deed from a true cause for Buddhahood. It fuses supreme philosophical insight with agonizing empathy for universal suffering.

figures.gloss: The Mahayana scripture (compiled c. 1st-2nd century CE) whose parable of the burning house and the three carts gave the vehicle-rhetoric this tradition is named for: a father lures his children from a burning house with the promise of three different carts, then gives every child the same single great cart — the separate vehicles revealed as skillful means toward one destination.
figures.facet: The literal source of the tradition's central metaphor: 'vehicle' (yana) as the operative term for a Buddhist path is not an abstract classification imposed later but this sutra's own narrative image, taken up as doctrine.
figures.provenance: Saddharmapundarika Sutra (Lotus Sutra), esp. chapter 3 (the Parable of the Burning House); Kumarajiva's Chinese translation (406 CE) as the most influential recension.

figures.gloss: The doctrine, drawn from the Lotus Sūtra, that the arahant's path, the bodhisattva's path, and the tantric path are not finally separate destinations but skillful presentations of a single vehicle: all beings, without exception, are ultimately bound for Buddhahood.
figures.facet: The resolution the three-vehicle scheme has been building toward: every distinction the roster holds between Theravada, Mahayana, and Vajrayana is, by this doctrine's own logic, provisional — real as pedagogy, dissolved at the level of final truth.
figures.provenance: Lotus Sūtra (Saddharmapundarika); Tiantai and Nichiren exegesis built specifically on this doctrine.

figures.gloss: The Buddha's pedagogical adaptivity: teachings pitched to the capacity of the specific hearer, true not as absolute metaphysical statement but as medicine matched to a particular disease — the working logic by which apparently contradictory teachings (self-effort vs. grace, emptiness vs. Buddha-nature) can all be taught by the same source.
figures.facet: The mechanism, not just the excuse, behind the whole vehicle-scheme: upaya is what allows Theravada renunciation, Mahayana universalism, and Vajrayana deity-yoga to coexist as one Buddha's teaching rather than as mutually falsifying claims.
figures.provenance: Lotus Sūtra (upaya-kausalya as its second chapter's named theme); widely elaborated across Mahayana sutra literature.

figures.gloss: The six (in some schemes ten) perfections — giving, ethical discipline, patience, energetic effort, meditative concentration, and wisdom — through which the bodhisattva's aspiration (bodhicitta) is trained into a realized career spanning countless aeons across multiple lifetimes.
figures.facet: The actual curriculum behind the vow: the roster already held the bodhisattva's aspiration and its supreme resolve, but not the graded training program that turns a one-time intention into a completed path.
figures.provenance: Prajnaparamita literature; Shantideva, Bodhicaryavatara (its chapters structured around several of the perfections).

figures.gloss: The 2nd-century-CE Indian philosopher-monk who founded the Madhyamaka ('Middle Way') school and gave Sunyata its rigorous dialectical form in the Mūlamadhyamakakārikā. Legend holds that he retrieved the Prajñāpāramitā sutras from the nāgas, serpent-beings who had guarded them in the deep since the Buddha's time.
figures.facet: The philosopher recast as a culture-hero: the legend of recovering hidden wisdom from the nāgas gives Nāgārjuna's severe logical treatise the same mythic shape as a fire-theft or a diving-for-treasure story — doctrine retrieved from the deep for humankind's use.
figures.provenance: Nāgārjuna, Mūlamadhyamakakārikā; the nāga-retrieval legend recorded in later Mahayana hagiography (e.g. as related by Bu-ston and other Tibetan historians).

figures.gloss: The ritual empowerment by which a qualified lama authorizes a disciple to practice a specific tantric deity — the mandatory gate of the entire Vajrayana vehicle. Without it, deity-yoga is regarded not merely as ineffective but as actively dangerous and doctrinally forbidden.
figures.facet: The permission structure the roster's Yidam and Guru entries both presuppose but neither names: tantric practice is not self-taught meditation technique but a transmitted authorization, withheld until formally granted.
figures.provenance: Tibetan Buddhist tantric literature (e.g. Guhyasamāja, Hevajra Tantra) and Lamrim empowerment procedure texts.

figures.gloss: The eighty-four tantric adepts of Indian legend — ferrymen, kings, hunters, and outcastes — who attained tantric realization amid ordinary or explicitly disreputable occupations, often through practices that deliberately violated caste and purity norms.
figures.facet: Vajrayana's ideal type, matching the roster's Theravada arahant and Mahayana bodhisattva: realization reached not by leaving the world (arahant) or returning to save it (bodhisattva) but by transgressing its rules from the inside, in the middle of low or forbidden work.
figures.provenance: Abhayadatta, Caturasiti-siddha-pravrtti (Lives of the Eighty-Four Siddhas); Keith Dowman's translation and study, Masters of Mahamudra.

figures.gloss: The 'Three Baskets' of monastic discipline (Vinaya), discourses (Sutta), and higher doctrine (Abhidhamma) — the closed canon Theravada Buddhism regards as the authentic recorded word of the historical Buddha, committed to writing in Sri Lanka in the 1st century BCE.
figures.facet: Theravada's scriptural anchor, standing in the roster where the Lotus Sūtra stands for Mahayana: each vehicle now holds its own charter document, rather than only the Mahayana and Vajrayana wings being textually represented.
figures.provenance: Pali Tipitaka; traditionally committed to writing at the Fourth Buddhist Council, Aluvihara, Sri Lanka, c. 29 BCE.

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: The Mahayana tradition explicitly defines its vast, inclusive Bodhisattva vow in direct theological opposition to what it critiques as the 'selfish' personal exit of the Arahant.
Intra-tradition bond: Vajrayana Deity Yoga is rendered completely invalid and devolves into mere idolatry unless it is grounded in the absolute realization of Sunyata.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Arahant is the living, breathing culmination of the Anatman principle, having experientially extinguished the illusion of an independent self.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the ultimate goal of ancient Indian shramana movements: a human who has completely burned away karmic defilements to achieve irreversible personal liberation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Avalokitesvara is the ultimate mythological personification of the abstract Bodhisattva ideal, embodying the active compassion that delays Nirvana.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both function as cosmic saviors who voluntarily enter the realm of suffering and sacrifice their own immediate bliss to secure the salvation of all beings.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are prophesied saviors destined to intervene in the suffering world to lead humanity toward final restoration, echoing the Maitreya figure.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both posit a profound, unnamable ultimate reality that is fundamentally empty of fixed form, yet acts as the generative matrix for all conditioned existence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions conceptualize the ultimate absolute as a paradoxical 'nothingness' (Ayin/Sunyata) from which all relative reality endlessly emanates.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: They mirror each other through opposition: the Pleroma is the divine 'fullness' generating all aeons, while Sunyata is the divine 'emptiness' realizing all forms as void.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Dharmakaya (Truth Body) of the Trikaya doctrine is explicitly identified with Sunyata—the empty, unconditioned nature of all Buddhas.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both posit a luminous, uncorrupted seed of absolute reality trapped within the defiled layers of material or karmic existence, waiting to be awakened.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Despite Buddhist rejections of a permanent Atman, Tathagatagarbha structurally functions much like Brahman—an eternal, inherent purity underlying the illusion of phenomenal reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both envision an inherently pure, enlightened core obscured by accumulated layers of karmic dirt that must be cleared to reveal its natural omniscience.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve the radical identification of a human practitioner with a divine being, though Vajrayana operates via mental visualization while Mesoamerican Ixiptla relied on physical ritual embodiment.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both systems feature an intimate, inextricably linked spiritual double or divine counterpart that the practitioner must integrate with to achieve ultimate realization.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Tantric 'Yab-Yum' depiction of Yidams in sexual union perfectly mirrors the union of Purusha (consciousness/method) and Prakriti (matter/wisdom) to generate enlightenment.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both act as the ultimate archetypal masters of esoteric transmission, holding the secret keys to inner alchemy that cannot be learned from dead texts.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an active, holy mentality or spiritual force that continuously guides the practitioner toward cosmic order and the absolute good.
GOLD SEAM: A documented laicization of the Mahayana vehicle — merit and awakening pursued by householders in the rice field, with monkhood and the temple economy stripped away.

BUDDHIST SEAM: the peak-entry transposes Vajrayana's mandala-and-deity technology into landscape — the Omine route is walked as the Womb and Diamond mandalas of Japanese mikkyo, and the ascetic ritually identifies with the wrathful deity (Fudo Myoo) as a yidam practitioner does.

BUDDHIST SEAM: Nichiren read himself into the Lotus Sutra as Bodhisattva Superior Practices, leader of the Bodhisattvas of the Earth, and SGI members take that role as their own collective identity — the Mahayana bodhisattva vow inherited as the movement's self-definition.

BUDDHIST SEAM: chanting works, in Nichiren doctrine, because Buddhahood is already innate and need only be called forth — the daimoku is the tathagatagarbha strand of Mahayana turned into a daily vocal technology.

A precise inversion of the root-teacher pattern: SGI abolished dependence on clergy altogether after the 1991 excommunication, yet re-centers devotion on the mentor-disciple bond with a lay president — the guru-function surviving, relocated outside priesthood entirely.

APPROPRIATION seam: Aum explicitly styled its secret teaching 'Vajrayana' and absolutized the root-guru doctrine into unconditional obedience to Asahara — an attested borrowing the parent tradition neither made nor endorses.
Direct reform-Buddhist descent: Won doctrine names Il-Won the original nature of all sentient beings — the Buddha-nature teaching inherited and set at the center of a modernized dharma.

Two answers to how dharma is transmitted: the Vajrayana root teacher absolutized as the sole living gate, against Sotaesan's deliberate laicization that opens the whole practice to everyone's working day.

APPROPRIATION seam: Aum named its own highest secret teaching 'Vajrayana' and used it as the doctrinal container suspending ordinary morality for the guru's ends — a borrowing of the vehicle's name and root-guru doctrine that historical Vajrayana Buddhism does not sanction.

Intra-tradition bond: the parable of the three carts in this sutra is the narrative origin of the vehicle-rhetoric the tradition is named for, and the One Vehicle is its own explicit doctrinal conclusion.
Intra-tradition bond: the burning-house parable is the sutra's own worked example of skillful means — a fiction of three different carts told to save the children, resolved into one.
Intra-tradition bond: upaya is the mechanism, ekayana the destination — the vehicles differ only as skillful means toward the single vehicle all are said to converge upon.
Intra-tradition bond: the one-vehicle doctrine's clearest practical embodiment — the bodhisattva path recast as the shared destiny of arahant and bodhisattva alike, not two competing goals.
Intra-tradition bond: the six perfections are bodhicitta's actual training curriculum — the one-time altruistic aspiration converted into a graded, multi-lifetime career.
Intra-tradition bond: the perfections are the concrete practice-path connecting the bodhisattva's initial vow to the eventual attainment of Buddhahood.
Intra-tradition bond: Nāgārjuna is the systematizer whose Mūlamadhyamakakārikā gives Sunyata its rigorous dialectical form — the roster's Sunyata entry is explicitly his legacy.
Intra-tradition bond: deity yoga is doctrinally forbidden without the empowerment that authorizes it — the rite is the unnamed gate the Yidam entry presupposes.
Intra-tradition bond: only a qualified living lama can confer empowerment — devotion to the guru and the rite of empowerment form one inseparable system of authorization.
Intra-tradition bond: the tantric adepts are deity-yoga's charter-practitioners, its ideal type realized amid ordinary or disreputable occupations rather than in the monastery.
Intra-tradition bond: Theravada's closed scriptural canon and the ideal type it describes and prescribes — charter document and its human goal.

APPROPRIATION seam: Aum's 'guru-energy initiations' mimicked the empowerment structure of tantric abhiseka — transmitting spiritual authority directly from teacher to disciple — while stripping out its lineage authorization and textual accountability, consistent with this tradition's other flagged Aum appropriation edges.
both posit an innate, positive potential within all persons — 'sprouts' of virtue / buddha-nature — that cultivation reveals rather than external imposition manufactures.
Both name an advanced being who postpones full liberation/return to a higher state in order to incarnate among the less advanced and serve their awakening.

Orthodox Buddhism holds Maitreya as a bodhisattva whose buddhahood lies in a remote future age; Yiguandao declares that wait already ended, with Milefo presently commanding the final salvation under the Eternal Mother.

dossier.essence: Not a separate religion but Buddhism's three great self-descriptions of the path: Theravada, the surviving Nikaya school of Sri Lanka and Southeast Asia claiming the elders' lineage; Mahayana, the 'great vehicle' movement emerging around the beginning of the common era in India and dominant in East Asia; and Vajrayana, the tantric 'diamond vehicle' formalized from about the 7th century CE and carried into Tibet, Mongolia, the Himalayas, and Japan (Shingon). Each vehicle crystallizes a different ideal of the perfected person — the arahant who ends rebirth, the bodhisattva who postpones final release for the sake of all beings, the tantric adept who enacts buddhahood now.
dossier.cosmos: All three share the common Buddhist frame — beginningless samsara, karma, rebirth, and liberation — but weight it differently: Theravada analyzes experience into momentary dhammas with nibbana as the unconditioned; Mahayana universalizes emptiness (sunyata), the three buddha-bodies (trikaya), and, in one influential strand, an innate buddha-nature in all beings; Vajrayana treats the world, properly seen, as already a pure mandala, and elaborates death itself (the bardo literature) as an opportunity for liberation.
dossier.practice: Theravada practice centers on monastic discipline, merit-making, and insight meditation; Mahayana adds the bodhisattva vow and perfections, vast devotional cults (Amitabha's Pure Land, Avalokiteshvara), and scholastic philosophy; Vajrayana requires empowerment (abhisheka) from a qualified guru, then deity-yoga — visualizing oneself as the yidam with mantra and mudra — under strict samaya bonds, the lama elevated to embodiment of the Buddha. Historically the vehicles stack rather than exclude: Vajrayana presupposes Mahayana, which presupposes the shared discipline.
dossier.canon: The Pali Tipitaka for Theravada; the Mahayana sutras (Prajnaparamita, Lotus, the Tathagatagarbha corpus) preserved chiefly in the Chinese and Tibetan canons; and the tantras (Guhyasamaja, Hevajra) with their commentarial and lamrim literature for Vajrayana.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds a tradition arguing with itself about the goal — perfection defined successively as escape, as compassionate return, and as transmutation of the present.
sources: Paul Williams, 'Mahayana Buddhism: The Doctrinal Foundations' (2nd ed., 2009)
David Snellgrove, 'Indo-Tibetan Buddhism: Indian Buddhists and Their Tibetan Successors' (1987)
